questions.question: A table of editable rows renders with rows.map((r, i) => <Row key={i} />). A user deletes the first row — and text they had typed into the second row's input now appears attached to the first row's data. What is the underlying mechanism?
choices: The delete used splice on the array already in state, mutating it in place, so React could not detect which row actually changed
React batched the delete with the pending input update, so both landed in one commit and the DOM patches were applied to the wrong nodes

key is React's identity contract across re-renders; with index keys every row's identity shifts after a delete, so React matches the old DOM — and its state — to the wrong data rows. The fix is a stable ID from the data
Inputs holding their own value are uncontrolled, violating the single-source-of-truth rule; converting them to controlled components makes the choice of key irrelevant
questions.explanation: React reconciles lists by key — it is the only signal saying which rendered item corresponds to which data item across renders. With key={index}, identity is positional: after deleting row 0, the old row-1 DOM (including whatever the user typed) is matched to the data that shifted into position 0. A stable ID from the data (a DB primary key) keeps identity attached to the item, not the slot. Mutating state in place produces a different symptom — the UI failing to update at all; batching only decides when a re-render happens, not how items are matched; and controlled inputs would merely mask the symptom while React kept reusing the wrong component instances and their state.

questions.question: A vote view increments the chosen row with F('votes') + 1, then returns HttpResponseRedirect(reverse('polls:results', ...)) instead of rendering the results template directly. What failure does the redirect prevent?
choices: Reading a stale vote count: within the same request the F() increment is not yet visible, so rendering directly would show the pre-vote total

Double submission: if the POST itself rendered the final page, refresh or the back button would re-send the POST and record the vote twice; redirecting makes the user's last navigation a harmless, repeatable GET
Leaking the submitted form fields into the URL, since a directly rendered response carries the POST body into the address bar
An uncacheable results page: browsers never cache responses to POST, so a redirect to a GET is required before the page can be cached
questions.explanation: This is the POST/Redirect/GET pattern: a non-idempotent request must never be the page the user ends up on. If the POST returned the results HTML, the browser's current history entry would be the POST itself — refresh and back-button navigation would silently resubmit it and duplicate the write. Redirecting leaves the user on a GET whose repetition is harmless. The staleness worry is unfounded — a request sees its own UPDATE, so a direct render would show the new count; the POST body never appears in the URL either way; and while POST responses are indeed rarely cacheable, cacheability is a side benefit, not the correctness problem this pattern exists to solve.

questions.question: During an incident, every target in an ALB target group starts failing its health checks — yet requests keep flowing to the broken targets and the "traffic is being served" dashboard stays green. What ALB behavior explains this, and what is the monitoring lesson?
choices: Connection draining: existing connections are allowed to finish for deregistration_delay (default 300s), so traffic tails off slowly rather than stopping

Fail-open: when every target in a group is unhealthy, the ALB routes to all of them anyway — a struggling target beats no target — so request volume is not proof of health; alarm on UnHealthyHostCount instead
Cross-zone load balancing: the load balancer nodes redistribute traffic to healthy targets in other Availability Zones, keeping total request volume constant
Health checks only gate the registration of new targets; targets already registered keep receiving traffic regardless of their check results
questions.explanation: The ALB fails open: health checks normally pull a failing target out of rotation, but when the entire group is unhealthy there is nothing left to prefer, so the ALB deliberately routes to all targets — a degraded backend may still serve some requests, while routing to nothing serves none. The operational consequence is that "requests are flowing" can be true while every backend is broken, so dashboards must alarm on health-check state (UnHealthyHostCount) and error rates, never on traffic volume. Draining applies to targets being deliberately deregistered, cross-zone balancing only helps if healthy targets exist somewhere, and under normal conditions health checks absolutely do gate routing for registered targets.

questions.question: To run three coding agents in parallel on one machine, you weigh (a) three separate clones of the repo against (b) three linked worktrees of one repo. Hard requirement: an agent's commit must be reviewable from your main checkout the moment it is made. What is the architectural argument for worktrees?
choices: Separate clones would share .git/HEAD, so a checkout in one agent's clone would silently move the other agents onto its branch
Worktrees give each agent a private ref namespace, so their feature branches cannot collide with yours during review
Worktrees are registered with the remote as a unit, so CI sees all three agents' branches from a single push

Linked worktrees share one object store and ref namespace behind separate working trees, HEADs, and indexes — a commit made at any desk is instantly visible from every other with no push, fetch, or remote round-trip; clones would need a fetch cycle before every review
questions.explanation: A linked worktree is a second desk in front of the same filing cabinet: files, HEAD, and index are per-worktree (agents cannot race each other's checkouts), while objects and refs are shared (a commit made in any worktree is immediately visible from the main one — git log --all and git diff main..feature/x work with zero synchronization). Clones isolate just as well but sever the shared store: every review would need a push from the agent's clone and a fetch into yours. The distractors invert the model — clones share nothing (each has its own HEAD, which is exactly the single-worktree race worktrees were built to fix), worktree refs are shared rather than private, and worktrees are local-only metadata that never reach the remote or CI.

questions.question: A DocumentAttachment table uses a GenericForeignKey — a content_type FK plus a plain object_id column — so one table can attach documents to any model with no schema change per new target. What integrity cost does this design accept, and where must it be repaid?
choices: object_id carries no database FK constraint, so deleting a target row leaves attachment rows dangling; the DB cannot cascade, and cleanup must be reimplemented in application code — e.g. a pre_delete receiver wired for every allowed target model
ContentType rows must be created by hand for each new model, so a forgotten row makes attachments to that model fail at runtime
Every new attachable model requires a migration to widen object_id to the new model's primary-key type
limit_choices_to on the content_type FK is enforced by the database on every insert, so the cost is write throughput rather than integrity
questions.explanation: A GFK trades referential integrity for schema flexibility: because object_id is a plain value column, the database has no idea the link exists — no cascade, no PROTECT, no orphan prevention. That guarantee doesn't disappear; it moves into code. And since deletes arrive from many paths (instance deletes, queryset deletes, FK cascades, admin actions), the robust repayment is a pre_delete signal receiver registered for every model in the attachment registry — a property of the models themselves rather than of each delete path. The distractors invert the design's facts: Django auto-creates ContentType rows at migrate time, object_id keeps one fixed type precisely so new targets need no migration, and limit_choices_to is evaluated only in forms and the admin — it enforces nothing at the database or API layer.

questions.question: A product owner starts embedding each story's full Given/When/Then acceptance criteria in the card itself, "so developers have everything up front." Judged against the 3Cs, what does this practice actually destroy?
choices: Nothing — it strengthens the story by making it Testable from day one, which is the T in INVEST

The card exists as a promise for a conversation; pre-answering the Confirmation on the card skips the conversation where the requirement's detail is meant to emerge, and freezes scope the team was supposed to negotiate — the AC belong to the Confirmation step, written after the discussion
It breaks Independent: acceptance criteria written up front create ordering dependencies between the stories in a sprint
It invalidates estimation, because a story with attached AC can no longer be sized until the criteria are removed
questions.explanation: In the 3Cs model the card is deliberately not the requirement — it is just enough text to identify the story and promise the Conversation, which is where the detail actually lives. Writing final AC on the card pre-answers that conversation, which is precisely the practice the format exists to protect: the story stops being Negotiable, and the Confirmation loses its role as the output of shared discovery (Three Amigos, Example Mapping) and becomes one author's upfront spec. Testability isn't the win here — INVEST's T asks that the outcome be observable, not that Gherkin be drafted before anyone talks. AC don't create inter-story dependencies, and estimation if anything gets easier with more detail — the casualty is negotiation and discovery, not sizing.

questions.question: A DRF ModelViewSet must turn DELETE /api/orders/{id}/ into a soft delete — set archived=True instead of removing the row. DRF splits every write action into an HTTP handler (destroy) and a one-line work hook (perform_destroy). Which override is correct, and why?
choices: Override destroy() and skip self.get_object() — resolving the full object is unnecessary when only one flag changes, and skipping it saves a query
Override the serializer's to_representation to hide archived rows — deletion semantics belong in the JSON layer so every view inherits them
Add @action(detail=True, methods=['delete'], url_path='archive') and leave destroy untouched, so clients opt into soft deletion explicitly
Override perform_destroy(instance) to archive instead of delete — it swaps what deletion means while DRF's destroy keeps the URL resolution, the get_object() 404 contract, and the 204 response
questions.explanation: The handler/hook split is a layering seam: destroy owns the HTTP contract (resolve the pk, 404 on unknown ids, return 204 No Content), while perform_destroy owns the single domain decision of what "delete" means. Soft deletion changes only the latter, so overriding the hook customizes the semantics and inherits the whole contract for free — the same split (create/perform_create, update/perform_update) holds across every write action. Overriding destroy while skipping get_object() is the classic gotcha: unknown ids flow past the 404 check and silently no-op. to_representation shapes output rows and cannot intercept a DELETE; and a separate /archive/ action changes the public API surface when the requirement was to redefine the resource's own DELETE.

questions.question: A story's acceptance criterion reads: "The backend caches the result in Redis for 60 seconds." It is binary and mechanically verifiable — yet it is flagged as an anti-pattern. What is the defect?
choices: It is not measurable enough: the criterion should also pin the Redis eviction policy and memory ceiling to be fully testable
Caching concerns are non-functional, so the statement belongs in the team's Definition of Done rather than in story-level AC

It specifies how instead of what: an AC is a contract on observable behavior, so it should state the user-visible guarantee — e.g. repeated identical searches within 60 seconds return consistent results in under 50 ms — and leave the mechanism to design docs
The format is wrong: a caching rule involves a trigger and an outcome, so it must be rewritten as a Given/When/Then scenario to be automatable
questions.explanation: AC describe what the system does from the outside — what a tester, PO, or customer would recognize as "the thing working" — never how it is built. "Caches in Redis for 60s" welds the story to one design: it cannot be verified without inspecting internals, it forbids equally valid implementations (in-process cache, CDN, precomputation), and it silently goes stale the day the design changes. Restated as an observable guarantee — a consistency window plus a latency threshold — the criterion survives any implementation and stays testable at the system boundary. Pinning eviction policy digs even deeper into implementation; the DoD holds team-wide gates that apply to every story, not story-specific behavior; and checklist-vs-Gherkin is orthogonal — the defect is the content, not the format.

questions.question: You delete legacy_ref — a NOT NULL column with no default — from the model, and the RemoveField migration is correctly staged post-deploy. Yet during the rollout, as soon as new-code servers come up, their INSERTs start failing. Why?
choices: RemoveField acquires an exclusive table lock that blocks concurrent inserts for the duration of the rollout window
Django's runtime model state diverges from the schema, so the ORM raises as soon as it encounters a column it no longer knows about

The new code's INSERTs no longer name legacy_ref, but the column still exists until the post-deploy drop — NOT NULL with no default rejects those writes; a pre-deploy step must first make the column nullable or give it a db_default
The staging is inverted: destructive operations must run pre-deploy so that both code versions see the same schema throughout the rollout
questions.explanation: The remove side of the coexistence window is symmetric to the add side: the moment the field leaves the model, Django stops listing it in INSERT statements — and the column is deliberately still in the schema, because the drop is staged after the old code is gone. A NOT NULL column with no default rejects any insert that doesn't name it, so the new code breaks against a column only the old code still fills. Field removal is therefore inherently two-phase: a pre-deploy AlterField making the column nullable (or persisting a db_default), then the post-deploy RemoveField — django-syzygy generates that pre-deploy half automatically; with django-safemigrate you stage it yourself. No lock is involved since the drop hasn't run yet; the ORM never introspects the schema at runtime; and moving destructive drops pre-deploy would break the old code instead — the mirror-image failure.

questions.question: "Customer" means one thing to Sales and another to Support, and specs keep tripping over it. A lead proposes a single company-wide definition to end the ambiguity. Why do DDD and CAS reject that move, and what do they prescribe instead?
choices: Total unification of a large domain's model is not feasible or cost-effective — a forced global definition matches nobody's real usage and quietly gets ignored; instead, fence the polysemy: two Contexts, each owning its own coherent definition, with the divergence documented in their Relationships
One definition is correct, but it must be owned by the larger of the two departments, with the other Context importing it read-only
Formal definitions are the mistake: concrete usage examples inside each story remove ambiguity without any dictionary maintenance
Retire the ambiguous word entirely and mint two newly invented terms to be used company-wide in speech, specs, and code
questions.explanation: DDD's central admission — the reason Bounded Contexts are its central pattern — is that one model for a whole organization is neither feasible nor cost-effective: language boundaries follow human culture, and both departments' meanings of "Customer" are correct within their own context. A forced company-wide definition produces the worst outcome — a term nobody actually uses — so the drift goes back underground where it is most expensive. The prescription is to stop fighting polysemy and fence it: one Context per coherent vocabulary, each owning its own Dictionary entry, with the relationship stated explicitly so cross-context Stories know to disambiguate (`term[Context]`). Imposing the bigger department's definition is unification wearing sharing clothes; examples without definitions leave the drift latent; and minting invented vocabulary recreates the same nobody-uses-it failure with extra confusion.
